5 min read
Treating everyone who enters your world like a valued customer
— regardless if money has changed hands — is a powerful
decision that creates many positive downstream consequences.
We’re not suggesting that you give away your work for free. That’s
not the point. Instead, we recommend that you change your
de�nition of a customer to more accurately re�ect how the world
really works.
To explain what we mean, we need to start with some context.
Distilled to its essential ingredients, the implicit goal for any
business system is to turn prospects into customers.
The typical de�nition of a customer is someone who has purchased
something. Everyone else who has actively raised his/her hand to
hear from you is a ‘lead’ or a ‘prospect’ (aka ‘prospective
customer’).
Strategic Upstream Decisions:
Customers are Created Before
They Buy Anything
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 11 --

For example, prospects are exposed to persuasive sales messages
to increase sales pressure, then there’s a moment when a sale
happens, money changes hands, and voilà — a new customer is
born.
If we adopt a linear view of reality, where A leads to B leads to C,
we assume customers are created by our deliberate efforts.
For example: Ad > Opt-In > Emails > O�er > Sale
To make that model work, we have to keep �lling the top of the
funnel knowing that the overwhelming majority of people we
attract won’t make it through to a sale.
However, in relationship-driven businesses, the transition from
prospect to customer usually is not linear or deliberate. It
emerges, interaction by interaction, over time in ways that are
unique to each individual.
To eliminate the counter-productive distinction between prospect
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 11 --

and customer, we’ve changed our de�nition of a customer from
someone who has purchased something to someone who is in our
care.
We take her interests seriously and, in the spirit of the Strategy of
Pre-Eminence, we accept �duciary responsibility for her within
our area of expertise — regardless if money has changed hands.
We contribute meaningful value to everyone in our care, guide
them, advise them, and protect their best interests knowing that
this orientation dramatically changes everything that follows.
Our marketing leads with generosity and empathy before a
transaction ever takes place and, as a result, we ‘win’ because our
prospects win �rst.
This isn’t a theoretical idea we tell ourselves in an effort to change
our mindset or feel better about the work we do. Instead, it’s a
more accurate re�ection of how the world really works.
When we treat everyone like customers, they begin to see
themselves as customers, and purchases emerge organically
as a result.
The implications of this behavior are real, where prospects
actually see themselves as customers because we’ve treated them
that way from the moment they entered our world.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 11 --

Landon hadn’t purchased anything when he sent us that email
(snippet above).
He had entered our world, lurked for a while, wandered around,
interacted with our content, and read many of our emails until he
was ready.
In his excitement, before his �rst transaction with us, he emailed
us to broadcast his intention.
Landon became a newly minted customer shortly after that email.
It’s impossible to know exactly when that moment will arrive for
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 11 --

any individual, and that’s perfectly OK. We don’t have to know
exactly when a transaction will happen to know that eventually,
for the right people at the right time, transactions will happen.
Rather than wait for that moment, we just assume it has already
happened from the beginning and we immediately embrace each
new person in our world as someone who is now in our care.
Like Llewellyn (he’s one of many examples we see weekly). He
came into our “Email Wonderland” on November 4, 2020 (see
image below).
Note: Blue dots are email not yet opened (there are none for
Llewellyn), green dots represent an opened email (all of them!),
and the gray circle with the small green dot inside, are opened
email and a link clicked.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 11 --

On May 1, 2021 — six month later — Llewellyn emailed us for the
�rst time. He had experienced an emergent moment — the non-
linear culmination of emails and interactions and experiences
slamming together in unpredictable ways caused him to reach out
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 11 --

to us.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 11 --

“First, I have a confession to make. I have not bought one of your
products YET( I plan to change this soon), but damn…”
Llewellyn will become a customer, maybe this week, maybe next.
When, speci�cally, is inconsequential. He already sees himself as
a customer and that changes everything for him and for us.
We know that this idea is easy to skip because it’s counter-
intuitive. However, trust us when we say that this is one of those
decisions that can change everything and we encourage you to
adopt this mindset.
Functionally, everything is easier when you treat everyone in your
care the same way. There’s no version of you that interacts with
prospects vs. a version of you that interacts with customers.
There’s just you (and that has powerful, trust-building effects your
audience will notice).
Beyond the functional bene�ts, however, is something far more
powerful. It’s impossible to put into words until you’ve
experienced it.
When you show up every day like everyone in your care is a
customer, don’t be surprised when that becomes your reality.
Please post your questions and comments below.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 11 --

P.S.
The names Amos Tversky and Daniel Kahneman may not mean anything
to you.
Their work introduced the notion of cognitive biases and illusions, and
their contributions to behavioral economics and decision-making have
been called “breathtakingly important.”
That’s how Michael Lewis described their collaboration, which is the
subject of his 2016 book, The Undoing Project.
The work produced from their collaboration was not only completely
di�erent than either of them had done on his own, but orders of
magnitude better.
In a touching, and wonderfully understated description of their work
together twenty-one years after Amos Tversky died, Kahneman described
their collaboration, saying:
“… we did work that was the best that either of us did over our long
careers, and both of us did pretty good work, but together we were quite
special…”
That last part of the sentence — “together we were quite special” — is
particularly important because of the underlying principle it represents.
Their brilliance wasn’t 1 + 1 = 2. Instead, ideas emerged from their
collaboration that neither of them could have achieved on his own.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 11 --

This lesson isn’t about two brilliant Israeli psychologists, but rather the
idea behind their brilliance — emergence — the outcome of the synergies
of parts interacting together.
There is untold power when we see everything as interconnectedness…
We can identify patterns and trends that produce positive emergent e�ects
(to amplify them), and negative emergent e�ects (to dissolve them
upstream).
“A system is a set of related components that work together in a
particular environment to perform whatever functions are required to
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 11 --

achieve the system’s objective.” — Donella Meadows (Thinking in
Systems)
When we understand and internalize this idea, we can engineer
environments and situations that produce positive emergence (we call this
worldbuilding, which is the subject of the next module). Instead of linear
thinking, where 1 + 1 = 2, we can think in terms of 1 + 1 = 5, 9, 15 or more.
It’s this emergent e�ect of “1 + 1 = 9” that allows us to create customers
before they buy anything, and even before there is an opportunity to buy.
Take a sec to digest the implications of this.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 11 --
